notes: These evals exercise the final adversarial review stage of the Kite pipeline. Each run is handed a complete feature dossier for the appsmith-v2 'App Export Download' feature: a blueprint (with corner cases), a system-design doc, a DELIBERATELY FLAWED set of code changes, and a plan file. The plan claims S1/S2 are committed and S3/S4/S5 are merely planned; the diff shows even S1/S2 are mis-built (zip assembled inline in the route handler, route writes straight to the DB, download streams with no status check) and contains an orphan usage-analytics module no scenario asked for. S3 (idempotency), S4 (ownership auth), and S5 (failure terminal state) are absent entirely. The skill should produce an adversarial review-report document, not fix anything or re-run the pipeline. Expectations assert the report's coverage check, orphan detection, per-scenario 'why it is unacceptable' arguments, kite-arch-compass principle citations, impact + concrete failure Gherkin, hunting for corner cases beyond the blueprint, and adherence to the prescribed report structure.

evals.name: full-feature-review
evals.prompt: All scenarios for the App Export Download feature in appsmith-v2 are committed and it's ready for the final review. The blueprint is files/blueprint.md, the system design is files/system-design.md, the full set of code changes is files/code-changes.diff, and the plan file is files/plan.md. Run the final review and produce the review report.
evals.expected_output: A standalone adversarial review-report document following the prescribed structure (Summary / Orphan changes / Scenario reviews / Missed corner cases / Recommended actions). The Summary states coverage as roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented and flags S3, S4, S5 as unimplemented. It identifies the usage_analytics module as an orphan change traceable to no scenario. For each scenario it argues WHY the implementation is unacceptable (not praise), cites specific kite-arch-compass principle violations (e.g. thin edges P2, layering P1, async work P19 for S1; status-gated download for S2; idempotency P15 for S3; least-privilege/never-trust-input P27/P29 for S4; resumable state machine/terminal states P16/P21 for S5), states the impact, and writes a concrete failure Gherkin (given/when/then). It hunts corner cases the blueprint never listed (e.g. files mutated mid-assembly, very large apps, artifact retention/cleanup). It does NOT fix the code or re-trigger implementation.
expectations: Produces a standalone review-report document that follows the prescribed structure exactly: a Summary section, an 'Orphan changes' section, a 'Scenario reviews' section with one subsection per scenario, a 'Missed corner cases' section, and a 'Recommended actions' section.

The Summary does a coverage check stating roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented and explicitly flags S3 (idempotent retry), S4 (ownership authorization), and S5 (assembly-failure terminal state) as unimplemented / having no associated code changes.

Detects the usage_analytics module (PostHog tracking + active_creators_daily rollup) as an orphan change that maps to no scenario in the blueprint, and calls it out as unrequested scope creep that should not be there.

For EACH scenario the review argues why the implementation is unacceptable with concrete reasons (adversarial framing), rather than praising or signing anything off; unimplemented scenarios are argued as unacceptable because nothing was built.

Cites specific kite-arch-compass principles behind the findings, including the inline zip assembly + DB access in the route handler violating thin-edges (Principle 2), layering (Principle 1), and do-slow-work-asynchronously (Principle 19); the missing idempotency for S3 (Principle 15); the missing server-side ownership check trusting a client-supplied workspace_id for S4 (least privilege Principle 27 and/or never-trust-input Principle 29); and the absent failed terminal state / non-resumable flow for S5 (Principle 16 and/or Principle 21).
Flags that the S2 download endpoint streams the artifact with no status==ready gate, so a failed or half-written export could be served as a valid download.

For at least the implemented scenarios, states the concrete impact ('because this is wrong, here is the consequence') AND writes a failure scenario as Gherkin (given/when/then) describing an input under which the implementation actually breaks.

Hunts missed corner cases BEYOND those the blueprint listed (Step 9) — e.g. app files changing mid-assembly, very large apps blocking the request thread / memory, or export-artifact retention and cleanup — not merely restating the blueprint's own S3-S5 corner cases.
Does NOT fix the code, rewrite the implementation, or re-trigger planning/implementation; any remediation appears only as advice under 'Recommended actions'.

evals.name: audit-changes-against-blueprint
evals.prompt: Audit these changes against the blueprint before I let this merge. files/blueprint.md is the spec, files/code-changes.diff is everything the team changed, files/system-design.md is the design we agreed on, and files/plan.md tracks scenario status. I want to know what's actually wrong with it — assume it's broken.
evals.expected_output: An adversarial audit report in the prescribed structure. It maps each chunk of the diff to the scenario it serves, finds the usage_analytics changes are an orphan, performs the coverage check (S3/S4/S5 unimplemented), and for each scenario explains why it is unacceptable with kite-arch-compass principle citations, impact, and a failure Gherkin. It also surfaces corner cases the blueprint missed. It produces a report for a human to act on and does not itself remediate.
expectations: Maps each chunk of the diff to the scenario it serves and reports coverage as roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented, explicitly naming S3, S4, and S5 as unimplemented.
Identifies the usage_analytics module as an orphan change traceable to no scenario and recommends it not be there (scope creep).
Argues, scenario by scenario, why each implementation is unacceptable rather than assessing whether it is good, holding the adversarial stance even where code superficially looks fine.

Cites specific kite-arch-compass principle numbers/names for the violations (e.g. Principle 2 thin edges and Principle 19 async for the inline assembly in the route; Principle 15 idempotency for S3; least-privilege / never-trust-input for the missing S4 ownership check; Principle 16 / Principle 21 for S5's missing failed terminal state).

States the consequence/impact of each major defect and writes at least one concrete failure case as Gherkin (given/when/then) where the implementation breaks (e.g. a retried POST creating duplicate exports, or a cross-workspace export succeeding).
Surfaces at least one corner case the blueprint did not list (e.g. concurrent file mutation during assembly, unbounded memory on large apps, or stale/orphaned export artifacts).
Delivers the findings as a review report for a human to decide on and does not itself fix the code or re-run the implementation/planning pipeline.
